## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — mimics operator onboarding journey under theurge control)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: crucible suite)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
75	
76	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
77	
78	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
79	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
80	
81	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
82	
83	Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.
84	
85	**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.
86	
87	## Suite-level fail-fast
88	
89	Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.
90	
91	## No-teardown decision
92	
93	The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:
94	
95	- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
96	- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
97	- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
98	- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.
99	
100	Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).
101	
102	## Operator scope
103	
104	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. Runbook lives in README.md release section.
105	
106	## Coupling to ₣A_
107	
108	Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.
109	
110	## Locked design decisions — pointers
111	
112	- **Depot identity collapse** (BBAAM, landed): RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
113	- **Cult-verb naming** (BBAAN/BBAAQ/BBAAR, landed): SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
114	
115	## References
116	
117	- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
118	- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
119	- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
120	- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)
121	
122	# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAe
123	
124	## Character
125	
126	First state-progressing fixture for the gauntlet test suite. Wraps §2 of the release-qualification sequence — canonical depot levy, governor mantle, retriever invest, director invest, IAM propagation wait. Sets the precondition-probe pattern subsequent state-progressing fixtures inherit.
127	
128	Service-tier — needs GCP credentials. Intent-only docket. Code-level planning happens at mount time.
129	
130	## Scope
131	
132	- New fixture covering §2 cases: canonical-depot-levy, governor-mantle, retriever-invest, director-invest, iam-propagation-wait
133	- Each case carries a precondition probe — refuses cleanly with operator-actionable diagnostic if expected state absent
134	- Set the precondition-probe pattern subsequent state-progressing fixtures inherit (concrete shape lives in BBAAd's helper module; this pace is the first to populate it)
135	- Disposition: StateProgressing
136	- Manifest registration
137	
138	## Cross-pace dependencies
139	
140	- gauntlet-engine-foundation landed (Disposition concept + probe-helper shape)
141	
142	## Out of scope
143	
144	- Onboarding-sequence fixture (subsequent pace)
145	- Suite assembly (gauntlet-suite-assembly)
146	- Live end-to-end cloud validation (BBAAX)
147	- Teardown — operator peeks post-success, then runs cleanup tabtargets manually

After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.

**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.

**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.

Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.

## Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

## Gauntlet test suite — design

`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.

**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.

**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).

**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:

1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — mimics operator onboarding journey under theurge control)
5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: crucible suite)

No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.

## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing

Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:

- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.

**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.

Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.

**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.

## Suite-level fail-fast

Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.

## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. Runbook lives in README.md release section.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions — pointers

- **Depot identity collapse** (BBAAM, landed): RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming** (BBAAN/BBAAQ/BBAAR, landed): SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.

## References

- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAe

First state-progressing fixture for the gauntlet test suite. Wraps §2 of the release-qualification sequence — canonical depot levy, governor mantle, retriever invest, director invest, IAM propagation wait. Sets the precondition-probe pattern subsequent state-progressing fixtures inherit.

Service-tier — needs GCP credentials. Intent-only docket. Code-level planning happens at mount time.

- Disposition: StateProgressing

## Cross-pace dependencies

- Suite assembly (gauntlet-suite-assembly)

17	// RBTDRB — precondition-probe helper for StateProgressing fixtures
18	//
19	// Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at entry.
20	// If the observed state matches the expected state the case body proceeds;
21	// otherwise the case returns a Fail verdict naming the expected state and
22	// the closest fixture that establishes it. The probe encapsulates state
23	// verification at the case level, so the engine does not need to track
24	// case history to support safe a-la-carte single-case rerun.
25	
26	use crate::rbtdre_engine::rbtdre_Verdict;
27	
28	/// A precondition probe attached to a StateProgressing case.
29	pub struct rbtdrb_Probe {
30	    /// Human-readable label for the precondition (e.g., "depot levied").
31	    pub name: &'static str,
32	    /// Check fn. Returns Ok(()) when the precondition holds; Err carries a
33	    /// diagnostic describing the observed state.
34	    pub check: fn() -> Result<(), String>,
35	    /// Operator-actionable next step naming the closest fixture or tabtarget
36	    /// that establishes the precondition.
37	    pub remediation: &'static str,
38	}
39	
40	/// Assert a probe at case entry.
41	///
42	/// On Ok(()) the precondition holds and the case body should proceed. On
43	/// Err(verdict) the case should return the verdict immediately — the verdict
44	/// names the unmet precondition and the remediation step.
45	pub fn rbtdrb_assert(probe: &rbtdrb_Probe) -> Result<(), rbtdre_Verdict> {
46	    match (probe.check)() {
47	        Ok(()) => Ok(()),
48	        Err(observed) => Err(rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
49	            "precondition '{}' not met: {}\nremediation: {}",
50	            probe.name, observed, probe.remediation
51	        ))),
52	    }
53	}

I have enough context. Here's the orientation:

## Context

**Heat ₣BB** rbk-mvp-3-release-qualification — racing, 20/27 done. Building `rbw-tP` (QualifyPristine), a third release-qualification tier that refuses to run unless marshal-zero was just committed. Operates as a **gauntlet TestSuite** of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification.

**Recent paddock work**: Currying, fixture-disposition design, no-teardown decision. `BBAAd` (just-landed) seeded the engine layer: `rbtdre_Disposition` (Independent/StateProgressing), `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate, and the `rbtdrb_probe` stub (`Probe` struct + `assert` helper).

**Pace ₢BBAAe** gauntlet-canonical-establish (rough, StateProgressing) — Wraps §2: canonical depot levy, governor mantle, retriever+director invest, IAM propagation. Each case carries a precondition probe. First fixture to populate the BBAAd probe shape. Service-tier (needs GCP creds).

## Naming Consideration

Silks "gauntlet-canonical-establish" matches docket scope precisely. Pristine-lifecycle uses internal throwaway prefixes (`prlc-`/`prlr-`, family `pristl`); by parallel, canonical-establish presumably installs fixture-internal canonical prefixes that *persist* for downstream gauntlet fixtures (onboarding-sequence, tadmor, etc.). "Canonical" distinguishes from "throwaway" — not from operator production.

## Proposed Approach

1. **New module** `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs` — classifier `k` (terminal exclusivity: a/c/d/e/f/i/m/p/b taken; k unused, mnemonic for canonical). Parallel structure to `rbtdrp_pristine.rs`: throwaway-prefix install helper → canonical-prefix install helper; family stem `canest` with auto-increment moniker.
2. **5 cases** in one section (`canonical-establish-arc`), each gated by a `rbtdrb_Probe`:
   - depot-levy: probes post-pristine state ready; installs canonical prefixes + moniker, levies, cross-checks project_id
   - governor-mantle: probes canonical depot present; mantles, copies governor RBRA to canonical path
   - retriever-invest: probes governor RBRA in place; invests, copies retriever RBRA, runs access-probe
   - director-invest: probes governor RBRA in place; invests, copies director RBRA, runs access-probe
   - iam-propagation-wait: probes both roles invested; polls until access-probe stable or times out
3. **Manifest** — new `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CANONICAL_ESTABLISH`, required colophons (depot levy, gov mantle, gov invest×2, access probe), wire into `rbtdrc_fixture_disposition` (StateProgressing) and `rbtdrc_sections_for_fixture`.
4. **Tabtarget plumbing** — register fixture in zipper; `tt/rbtd-r.Run.canonical-establish.sh` and `tt/rbtd-s.SingleCase.canonical-establish.sh` follow existing per-fixture launcher pattern.
5. **Tests** `rbtdtk_canonical.rs` — case-registry shape, fixture-name disposition lookup, probe Ok/Err paths. No live cloud assertions (service-tier execution is operator-driven).

**Execution strategy**: Sequential opus — multi-file orchestration with cross-references between manifest, crucible-dispatch, and the new fixture. Not parallelizable.

**Open questions before I start:**
- **Canonical prefix values** — propose `canc-`/`canr-` (parallel to pristine's `prlc-`/`prlr-`)? Or pull from a future RBRR canonical-prefix field?
- **iam-propagation-wait shape** — bounded poll loop on access-probe success (e.g., 5-min timeout, 10-sec interval), or fixed sleep + single probe? The §2 sequence in BBAAB notes "IAM propagation wait" but doesn't specify mechanics.
- **Single section vs gate+arc split** — pristine has `pristine-lifecycle-gate` (marshal-zero attestation) + `pristine-lifecycle-arc` (3 cases). Canonical-establish has no gate-attestation analogue — preceding pristine-lifecycle is the gate. Single-section feels right; confirm?
- **Probe for case 1** — what state must hold? Throwaway depot torn down (DELETE_REQUESTED in fact-file)? Or is presence of throwaway prefixes from pristine the valid precondition, with "canonical not yet established" being implicit?

Ready to proceed once these are resolved, or I can take reasonable defaults and flag decisions in the commit.

**Character**: First state-progressing fixture for the gauntlet test suite. Wraps §2 of the release-qualification sequence. Sets the precondition-probe pattern subsequent state-progressing fixtures inherit. Service-tier (GCP credentials). Intent-only docket — code-level planning at mount time.

**Scope**:
- New fixture, originally listed cases: canonical-depot-levy, governor-mantle, retriever-invest, director-invest, **iam-propagation-wait** (questioned below)
- Each case carries a precondition probe — refuses cleanly with operator-actionable diagnostic if expected state absent
- First populator of the BBAAd `rbtdrb_Probe` shape
- Disposition: **StateProgressing**
- Manifest registration

**Cross-pace dependencies**: BBAAd gauntlet-engine-foundation landed (Disposition enum, fail-fast policy gate, probe-helper shape).

**Out of scope**: onboarding-sequence fixture (next pace); suite assembly; live end-to-end cloud validation (BBAAX); teardown (operator peeks post-success then runs cleanup tabtargets manually — no-teardown is a paddock-locked decision).

**Paddock framing that matters here**:
- Gauntlet is a TestSuite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script
- StateProgressing → engine refuses keep-going, fail-fast required
- Per-case probes enable safe a-la-carte single-case rerun
- Run on a release branch; commits during the run are first-class; stop on very first failure cleanly
- Inspection-after-success is genuine operator value; no automatic teardown

## (2) iam-propagation-wait — you're right to challenge

I was carrying it forward from the paddock's §2 list without scrutiny. Looking at how pristine-lifecycle's SA cycle works (`rbtdrp_sa_cycle_impl`): it calls invest, then immediately runs access-probe — no separate wait. If propagation isn't ready, **the tabtarget should be the place that handles it** (either invest blocks until usable, or access-probe retries internally). A test fixture's job is to call the tabtarget and assert the outcome — not to bake operational waiting into the test sequence.

The "IAM propagation wait" framing is an artifact of the original §2 sequence written before the per-tabtarget contract was tightened. The disposition-of-truth: each invoked tabtarget runs to completion or fails in reasonable time.

**Recommendation**: drop iam-propagation-wait as a case. If propagation latency is real, fix is at the tabtarget layer (invest waits, or access-probe retries) — not here. The remaining four cases (depot-levy, governor-mantle, retriever-invest, director-invest) plus their probes are sufficient for §2 establishment.

If we want a stability check, the right shape is: each invest case ends by calling access-probe and that's the case's pass criterion (already pristine-lifecycle's pattern).

## (1) and (3) confirmed

- Canonical prefixes: `canc-` / `canr-`, family stem `canest`
- Single section: `canonical-establish-arc` (no gate-attestation — pristine-lifecycle is the gate)

## Updated proposal

**4 cases** in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs`:

| Case | Probe (precondition) | Body |

#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBTE CLI - Theurge test engine CLI entry point with differential furnish
#
# Sourced by rbtw_workbench.sh. Provides rbte_dispatch() for command routing.
# Light furnish: buv + burd + buz + rbz + rbte (all commands)
# Heavy furnish: regime + constants + OAuth + IAM + Payor (probe only)

set -euo pipefail

ZRBTE_CLI_DIR="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

######################################################################
# Light furnish — source and kindle for all commands

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/burd_regime.sh"
source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buz_zipper.sh"
source "${ZRBTE_CLI_DIR}/../rbz_zipper.sh"
source "${ZRBTE_CLI_DIR}/rbte_engine.sh"

zbuv_kindle
zburd_kindle
zbuz_kindle
zrbz_kindle
zrbte_kindle

######################################################################
# Heavy furnish — regime + full module chain for access probe

zrbte_furnish_probe() {
  zburd_sentinel

  local z_rbk="${ZRBTE_CLI_DIR}/.."
  source "${z_rbk}/rbrr_regime.sh"
  source "${z_rbk}/rbrp_regime.sh"
  source "${z_rbk}/rbcc_Constants.sh"
  source "${z_rbk}/rbgc_Constants.sh"
  source "${z_rbk}/rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh"
  source "${z_rbk}/rbgo_OAuth.sh"
  source "${z_rbk}/rbgu_Utility.sh"
  source "${z_rbk}/rbgi_IAM.sh"
  source "${z_rbk}/rbgp_Payor.sh"
  source "${z_rbk}/rbgv_AccessProbe.sh"

  source "${RBBC_rbrr_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source ${RBBC_rbrr_file}"
  zrbrr_kindle

  # Payor probe is depot-agnostic: skip RBRR enforcement so it runs against
  # blank-template RBRR. zrbdc_kindle still runs to derive RBDC_PAYOR_RBRO_FILE
  # (credential path needed by the probe); depot-identity RBDC_* values it
  # also composes are unread on the Payor path. Mirrors BBAAS pattern in
  # rbgp_cli.sh:56-60 for rbgp_depot_list.
  local z_role="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  if test "${z_role}" != "payor"; then
    zrbrr_enforce
  fi
  zrbcc_kindle
  zrbdc_kindle
  zrbgc_kindle
  zrbgo_kindle
  zrbgu_kindle
  zrbgi_kindle
  zrbgp_kindle
  zrbgv_kindle
}

######################################################################
# Dispatch

rbte_dispatch() {
  local z_command="$1"
  shift

  zburd_sentinel
  zrbte_sentinel

  case "${z_command}" in

    rbtd-b)
      rbte_build "$@"
      ;;

    rbtd-t)
      rbte_test "$@"
      ;;

    rbtd-r)
      rbte_run "$@"
      ;;

    rbtd-s)
      local z_frontispiece="${BURD_TOKEN_2:-}"
      case "${z_frontispiece}" in
        TestSuite)   rbte_suite "$@" ;;
        SingleCase)  rbte_single "$@" ;;
        *)           buc_die "Unknown frontispiece for rbtd-s: ${z_frontispiece} (expected TestSuite|SingleCase)" ;;
      esac
      ;;

    rbtd-ap)
      zrbte_furnish_probe
      rbte_probe "$@"
      ;;

    *)
      buc_die "Unknown command: ${z_command}"
      ;;

  esac
}

# eof

#!/bin/bash

# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBTE Engine - Theurge test engine implementation module
#
# BCG module providing kindle/sentinel/public functions for theurge
# Rust build, test, and orchestration commands.

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion guard
test -z "${ZRBTE_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZRBTE_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Kindle

zrbte_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "rbte already kindled"

  local z_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"

  readonly RBTE_MANIFEST="${z_dir}/Cargo.toml"
  readonly ZRBTE_BINARY="${z_dir}/target/debug/rbtd"

  # Theurge-own colophons (routed by rbte_dispatch, not the RBK zipper)
  readonly ZRBTE_COLOPHONS="rbtd-ap"

  # Combined manifest: RBK zipper colophons + theurge-own colophons
  readonly ZRBTE_FULL_MANIFEST="${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST} ${ZRBTE_COLOPHONS}"

  # Suite-to-fixture mappings
  ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=("enrollment-validation" "regime-validation" "regime-smoke" "handbook-render")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "four-mode" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "four-mode" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")

  readonly ZRBTE_KINDLED=1
}

######################################################################
# Sentinel

zrbte_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbte not kindled - call zrbte_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

zrbte_build_binary() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  test -x "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" || buc_die "Theurge binary not found: ${ZRBTE_BINARY}"
}

zrbte_resolve_suite() {
  local z_suite="$1"
  case "${z_suite}" in
    fast)     echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[*]}" ;;
    service)  echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE[*]}" ;;
    crucible) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE[*]}" ;;
    complete) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE[*]}" ;;
    *)        buc_die "Unknown suite: ${z_suite} (expected fast|service|crucible|complete)" ;;
  esac
}

######################################################################
# Public functions

rbte_build() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  buc_success "Theurge built"
}

rbte_test() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Testing theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo test --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo test failed"
  buc_success "All theurge tests passed"
}

rbte_run() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_fixture="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  test -n "${z_fixture}" || buc_die "No fixture imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbtd-r.Run.tadmor.sh)"

  zrbte_build_binary

  buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBTE_FULL_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
}

rbte_suite() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_suite="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  test -n "${z_suite}" || buc_die "No suite imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh)"

  local z_fixture_list
  z_fixture_list="$(zrbte_resolve_suite "${z_suite}")"

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_count=0
  local z_fixture
  for z_fixture in ${z_fixture_list}; do
    buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
    "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBTE_FULL_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
    z_count=$((z_count + 1))
  done

  buc_success "Suite '${z_suite}' complete (${z_count} fixtures)"
}

rbte_single() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_fixture="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  test -n "${z_fixture}" || buc_die "No fixture imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbtd-s.SingleCase.tadmor.sh)"

  zrbte_build_binary

  local z_case="${1:-}"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" single "${ZRBTE_FULL_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}" ${z_case:+"${z_case}"}
}

rbte_probe() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_role="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  test -n "${z_role}" || buc_die "No role imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbtd-ap.AccessProbe.governor.sh)"

  local z_iterations=5
  local z_delay_ms=1500

  case "${z_role}" in
    governor|director|retriever)
      buc_step "JWT SA access probe: ${z_role}"
      rbgv_jwt_sa_probe "${z_role}" "${z_iterations}" "${z_delay_ms}"
      buc_success "${z_role} JWT access probe passed"
      ;;
    payor)
      buc_step "Payor OAuth access probe"
      source "${RBBC_rbrp_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source RBRP: ${RBBC_rbrp_file}"
      zrbrp_kindle
      zrbrp_enforce
      rbgv_payor_oauth_probe "${z_iterations}" "${z_delay_ms}"
      buc_success "Payor OAuth access probe passed"
      ;;
    *)
      buc_die "Unknown access-probe role: ${z_role} (expected governor|director|retriever|payor)"
      ;;
  esac
}

# eof

22	// ── Verdict ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
23	
24	/// Verdict from a single test case execution.
25	pub enum rbtdre_Verdict {
26	    Pass,
27	    Fail(String),
28	    Skip(String),
29	}
30	
31	// ── Disposition ────────────────────────────────────────────────
32	
33	/// Fixture disposition controls per-fixture mode policy at the engine layer.
34	///
35	/// `Independent` — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine
36	/// permits keep-going mode for surveying.
37	///
38	/// `StateProgressing` — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1.
39	/// Engine refuses keep-going mode (a failed case leaves a broken precondition for
40	/// the next case, so continuing is incoherent). Fail-fast is forced.
41	#[derive(Copy, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Debug)]
42	pub enum rbtdre_Disposition {
43	    Independent,
44	    StateProgressing,
45	}
46	
47	/// Resolve the effective fail-fast bool from a disposition and a caller's
48	/// requested keep-going mode. Returns Err if the request is incompatible with
49	/// the disposition (StateProgressing + keep-going).
50	///
51	/// Centralizes the keep-going / fail-fast policy at the engine boundary so all
52	/// callers funnel through one place.
53	pub fn rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast(
54	    disposition: rbtdre_Disposition,
55	    keep_going_requested: bool,
56	) -> Result<bool, String> {
57	    match (disposition, keep_going_requested) {
58	        (rbtdre_Disposition::StateProgressing, true) => Err(
59	            "rbtd: keep-going mode refused for StateProgressing fixture — \
60	             a failed case leaves a broken precondition for the next case, \
61	             so continuing is incoherent. Run individual cases via the \
62	             SingleCase tabtarget if you want to survey."
63	                .to_string(),
64	        ),
65	        (rbtdre_Disposition::StateProgressing, false) => Ok(true),
66	        (rbtdre_Disposition::Independent, keep_going) => Ok(!keep_going),
67	    }
68	}
69	
70	// ── Case and Section ───────────────────────────────────────────
71	
72	/// A named test case with a function that receives its isolated temp directory.
73	/// The `name` field holds the raw stringified function name (from the `case!` macro).
74	pub struct rbtdre_Case {
75	    pub name: &'static str,
76	    pub func: fn(&Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict,
77	}
78	
79	/// A named group of test cases, printed as a section header during execution.
80	pub struct rbtdre_Section {
81	    pub name: &'static str,
82	    pub cases: &'static [rbtdre_Case],
83	}
84	
85	/// Case registration macro. Derives case name from function name via `stringify!`.
86	/// Compiler enforces uniqueness — duplicate function names won't compile.
87	#[macro_export]
88	macro_rules! case {
89	    ($func:path) => {
90	        $crate::rbtdre_engine::rbtdre_Case {
91	            name: stringify!($func),
92	            func: $func,
93	        }
94	    };
95	}
96	
97	// ── Colors ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
98	
99	/// Terminal color codes, empty strings when color is disabled.
100	pub struct rbtdre_Colors {
101	    pub green: &'static str,
102	    pub red: &'static str,
103	    pub yellow: &'static str,
104	    pub white: &'static str,
105	    pub reset: &'static str,
106	}
107	
108	/// Detect terminal color support from TERM environment variable.
109	pub fn rbtdre_detect_colors() -> rbtdre_Colors {
110	    match std::env::var("TERM") {
111	        Ok(term) if !term.is_empty() && term != "dumb" => rbtdre_Colors {
112	            green: "\x1b[1;32m",
113	            red: "\x1b[1;31m",
114	            yellow: "\x1b[1;33m",
115	            white: "\x1b[1;37m",
116	            reset: "\x1b[0m",
117	        },
118	        _ => rbtdre_Colors {
119	            green: "",
120	            red: "",
121	            yellow: "",
122	            white: "",
123	            reset: "",
124	        },
125	    }
126	}

attachment.prompt: I have fully delegated the design of theurge testing to you.  I was a little surprised to see lists of tabtarget colophons in your code.  Are fixtures/ cases actually interleaving calling tabtargets and other routines to check fact files and the like between tabtarget calls? content.text:
Pausing the build to address your question — this is design feedback that should land before I run anything.

## Yes, fixtures interleave tabtarget calls with non-tabtarget routines

This is the established pattern from `rbtdrp_pristine`. A case is a saga: invoke tabtarget → inspect its output → mutate state → invoke another tabtarget → cross-check, etc. For example, my `rbtdrk_depot_levy` does:

1. `rbtdrk_install_canonical_prefixes` — rewrite `rbrr.env`, `git add` + commit (filesystem + git, not a tabtarget)
2. invoke `rbw-dl` (depot_list) — capture BURV output
3. `rbtdrk_pick_next_moniker` — read fact files from BURV output dir, compute next free `canest` suffix
4. `rbtdrk_install_depot_moniker` — write moniker to `rbrr.env`, commit
5. invoke `rbw-dL` (depot_levy)
6. invoke `rbw-dl` again — capture post-levy fact files
7. read `<moniker>.depot-project` fact file → `fact_project_id`
8. `rbtdrk_compose_project_id` — read `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` from `rbrr.env`, format `<prefix>d-<moniker>`
9. assert `composed == fact_project_id` — cross-check that the kindle derivation (RBDC) and the payor's actual creation agree

That's three tabtarget invocations interleaved with six filesystem/git operations.

**Why this shape is load-bearing:**
- The orchestrator chooses arguments for downstream tabtargets based on upstream outputs (e.g., the moniker has to be unique-per-run; only the orchestrator can compute that).
- Cross-tabtarget consistency checks (RBDC compose vs payor fact, divest-deletes-RBRA contract from BBAAN) are properly orchestrator-level — no single tabtarget can verify them.
- Fact files are the documented wire format between tabtargets and orchestrator code (BURV output dir → `<name>.<ext>` files with single-line values).

## What the colophon lists are for

There are two distinct uses:

**(1) Inside case bodies** (e.g., `rbtdri_invoke_global(ctx, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_DEPOT_LEVY, …)`) — these are the actual tabtarget invocations. The colophon constants are just typed names instead of bare strings.

**(2) In `rbtdrm_required_colophons`** (the per-fixture `Some(&[…])` list) — this is a **startup integrity check**, not a runtime enumeration. At fixture launch, the rbtd binary's `rbtdrm_verify` walks the project's zipper manifest (the space-separated string of all enrolled colophons) and refuses to start if any colophon the fixture might invoke is missing. Catches "tabtarget moved/deleted/typo" before any live cloud calls happen.

So the manifest list is "what *could* be called" and the case bodies are "what *gets* called." They're independent — the manifest list is a fail-fast guard, not a contract on call order or count.

## Should I have second-guessed the pattern?

No, I don't think so — the interleaving is the only way to do orchestrator-level cross-checks, and it's what pristine-lifecycle established. But if you'd prefer a more black-box shape (each case = one tabtarget invocation with binary pass/fail, no orchestrator-side state inspection), that's a real architectural choice and I should pause.

Want me to proceed with build + tests as written, or do you want to discuss the pattern first?

message.content: That is intriguing...  I do like it.  Is this 'self checking'?  If a case tries to use a tabtarget that is not in its manifest, is there a failure?  You can proceed with build and test as written.

content.text:
Honest answer: **partially self-checking, with a real gap.**

**What IS checked:**
- At fixture startup, `rbtdrm_verify` walks the declared list and refuses to launch if any declared colophon is missing from the zipper manifest. So if you declare a colophon that's been deleted/moved, you fail-fast before any cloud calls.
- At runtime, `rbtdri_find_tabtarget_global` looks up the tabtarget by colophon and returns an error if no `tt/<colophon>.*.sh` file exists. So invoking a non-existent colophon fails immediately.

**What is NOT checked:**
- The declared list doesn't constrain runtime invocations. If a case calls `rbtdri_invoke_global(ctx, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_DEPOT_UNMAKE, …)` but `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_DEPOT_UNMAKE` isn't in that fixture's required-colophons list, **nothing complains** — the call just runs, because the colophon does exist in the zipper.

So the declared list is currently "asserted superset" not "exhaustive subset." If a maintainer adds a new tabtarget call to a case but forgets to update `rbtdrm_required_colophons`, the integrity check stays green even though the declaration is now stale.

**Concretely**: my canonical-establish list omits `RBTDRM_COLOPHON_DEPOT_UNMAKE` (correct — the fixture doesn't unmake) but if I had typo'd `rbtdrk_depot_levy` to call `_DEPOT_UNMAKE` instead of `_DEPOT_LEVY`, the build wouldn't catch it. The case would just run unmake and probably fail with a "no canonical depot to unmake" diagnostic at the unmake tabtarget itself.

**Could be tightened** by walking each fixture's case bodies at compile time (e.g., a static-analysis-style enumeration) or by routing all `rbtdri_invoke_global` calls through a wrapper that asserts `colophon ∈ rbtdrm_required_colophons(current_fixture)`. Both are real follow-up work, not this pace.

Proceeding with build + tests now.

content.text:
Nothing pending — `30bc2b41` is the BBAAe pace commit, tree is clean.

## Runtime check — yes, safe and surgical

The change funnels through one chokepoint: all tabtarget invocations go through three primitives (`rbtdri_invoke`, `rbtdri_invoke_global`, `rbtdri_invoke_imprint`). Wrappers like `rbtdrk_invoke_logged` and `rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit` delegate to those primitives, so instrumenting the three primitives catches everything transitively without touching case bodies.

**Two checks, same chokepoint:**

| Check | Direction | When |
|-------|-----------|------|
| Positive: invoked → declared | At each invocation: refuse if colophon ∉ declared list | Always (full fixture and single-case) |
| Negative: declared → invoked | At fixture end: fail if any declared colophon went unused | Only on full-fixture **and** zero failures |

**Surface area:**

1. `rbtdri_Context` gains two fields — `declared_colophons: HashSet<&'static str>` (populated at fixture init from `rbtdrm_required_colophons`) and `used_colophons: HashSet<&'static str>` (populated by the primitives).
2. The three primitives gain a 2-line guard: refuse if not declared, record on success.
3. `main.rs::run_suite` constructs `declared_colophons` at init, and after `run_sections` returns success, computes `declared - used` and fails non-empty.
4. `main.rs::run_single` constructs the context with an empty declared set or a `check: false` flag — exhaustiveness skipped, positive check still on if you want it (single-case still benefits from "you invoked something undeclared").

**Wrinkles (manageable, not blockers):**

- **Failure-path skipping** for the negative check is mandatory. If any case failed, the run is already failing — "unused declared colophon" is noise. Gate exhaustiveness on `result.failed == 0`.
- **Single-case mode** can't satisfy exhaustiveness by construction. Skip the negative check; keep the positive.
- **Conditional invocations**: I don't see any in existing fixtures (every declared colophon is unconditionally called in the success path), but turning on the negative check serves as the audit. If anything trips, that's a real stale declaration — exactly what we want to catch.
- **Crucible-fixture lifecycle invocations** (CHARGE/QUENCH/CRUCIBLE_ACTIVE) happen in main.rs, not in cases. They go through the same primitives, so they'd be tracked and counted toward exhaustiveness automatically — that's fine because they're in the declared list for crucible fixtures.

**Two-phase rollout** if you want to be extra-cautious:
- Phase 1: positive check only — zero risk of false positives, immediate value.
- Phase 2: negative check — running the existing TestSuites becomes the audit; if anything fails, that's a real maintenance signal.

Both can land in a single pace; the negative check's potential audit cost is the only reason to consider splitting.

**One small caveat I'd want flagged in the docket**: the positive check moves "tabtarget moved/typo'd" from "fails with bash error at the failed tabtarget" to "fails earlier with theurge-level message naming the fixture and the undeclared colophon" — better diagnostics, but the operator-facing message changes shape. Worth noting for anyone reading old test traces.

Want me to slate this as a follow-up pace? I can draft the docket if so.

# jjezs_slate theurge-manifest-coverage-check

## Character

Tighten rbtdrm's per-fixture required-colophons list from "asserted superset" to "exhaustive subset". Two runtime checks, both at the rbtdri_invoke_* primitive chokepoint:

- Positive: every invoked colophon must be in the fixture's declared list — refuse otherwise.
- Negative: every declared colophon must have been invoked at least once on a successful full-fixture run — fail otherwise.

Surgical refactor; no case-body edits expected. The audit risk for the negative check surfaces at turn-on; existing TestSuite runs serve as the audit gate.

## Scope

- rbtdri_Context tracks declared and used colophon sets
- All three invoke primitives (rbtdri_invoke, _global, _imprint) refuse undeclared invocations and record successful ones
- run_suite enforces the negative check after run_sections returns success (gated on result.failed == 0 — failure paths suppress the check)
- run_single enforces positive only — single-case cannot satisfy exhaustiveness by construction
- Unit tests covering both check directions and the single-case exemption
- Operator-facing diagnostics name the fixture and the offending colophon

## Cross-pace dependencies

None. The check applies to all theurge fixtures, independent of gauntlet assembly.

## Out of scope

- Imprint coverage (which nameplate a per-imprint colophon was called with), argument patterns, compile-time/static analysis
- Phase-split into two paces — implement both checks together; the TestSuite run is the validation gate
- Any fixture-level declaration edits — the implementation should pass clean against current declarations or surface a real stale entry as a separate find

- `probe-diagnostics` (1) — unmet-probe stderr contains "precondition '%s' not met:" + "remediation:"

content.text:
## Slate confirmed

`₢BBAAj theurge-manifest-coverage-check` is enrolled.

## Calibrant system (BBAAh + BBAAi)

Two-pace pair that builds a black-box test layer over the rbtd binary using synthetic fixtures with deterministic verdicts:

**BBAAh** (Rust): `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` registers four fixtures — `calibrant-verdicts`, `calibrant-fail-fast`, `calibrant-progressing`, `calibrant-sentinel`. The `calibrant` suite sequences three of them; sentinel-after-fail proves cross-fixture fail-fast.

**BBAAi** (bash): `rbtt_testbench.sh` drives the binary and asserts CLI exit codes, stderr shapes, fail-fast policy, disposition gate, probe diagnostics, suite fail-fast, CLI usability — 14 cases across 6 sections.

The calibrant layer's purpose is exactly the niche my BBAAj feature fits into: **what the operator sees when something is misdeclared**.

## Additions to prove BBAAj works

### BBAAh (Rust calibrant additions)

**One synthetic theurge-internal tabtarget** — a bare `exit 0` no-op the synthetic fixtures invoke without any cloud or filesystem side-effects. Naming at mount time (something under the `rbtd-l*` colophon family for parity with `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs`).

**Three new fixtures** (all Independent disposition):

| Fixture | Manifest declares | Case body invokes | Expected verdict |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| `calibrant-coverage-aligned` | `[noop]` | `noop` | Pass (baseline) |
| `calibrant-coverage-undeclared` | `[]` | `noop` | Fixture FAIL with positive-check diagnostic naming `noop` |
| `calibrant-coverage-unused` | `[noop]` | nothing | Fixture FAIL with negative-check diagnostic naming `noop` |

These are NOT added to the `calibrant` suite — they're invoked standalone by bash so suite-fail-fast assertions stay clean. Rust unit tests pin only that the fixtures and tabtarget are wired; the verdict assertions live in BBAAi.

### BBAAi (bash blackbox additions)

**One new section, four cases** (extending the existing 14-case / 6-section catalog to 18-case / 7-section):

| Section | Case | Asserts |
|---------|------|---------|
| `manifest-coverage` | `coverage-aligned-passes` | `Run.calibrant-coverage-aligned` exits 0 |
| | `coverage-undeclared-fails` | `Run.calibrant-coverage-undeclared` exits non-zero; stderr matches the positive-check diagnostic pattern (fixture name + offending colophon) |
| | `coverage-unused-fails` | `Run.calibrant-coverage-unused` exits non-zero; stderr names the unused colophon |
| | `coverage-single-case-skips-negative` | `SingleCase.calibrant-coverage-unused` of its only case exits 0 — proves single-case exemption |

The single-case exemption case is the most important one — it's the only test that exercises asymmetric check behavior between full-fixture and single-case modes. Without it, an implementation that runs the negative check in single-case mode would pass-by-accident in the suite (because the suite never SingleCases).

## Sequencing

These additions depend on BBAAj landing. Three options for how to thread them:

1. **Reslate BBAAh and BBAAi in place** — extends each docket's scope. Clean if BBAAj lands first; awkward if BBAAh/BBAAi land before BBAAj.
2. **Separate follow-up paces** (e.g., `BBAAk calibrant-coverage-fixtures`, `BBAAl calibrant-coverage-driver`) that depend on BBAAj + BBAAh + BBAAi. Keeps each pace's scope crisp, costs two more paces.
3. **Single follow-up pace** combining the Rust + bash additions, depending on all three. Compact but blends layers (calibrant pattern is normally split Rust/bash).

I'd recommend option 2 — keeps the original calibrant paces from creeping in scope, makes the dependency graph explicit, and the work decomposes naturally along the existing Rust/bash boundary. Want me to slate those two follow-ups?

content.text:
Two follow-up paces, parallel to the BBAAh / BBAAi shape. Both depend on the foundation paces landing first.

### Pace A: `calibrant-coverage-foundation` (Rust extension)

```
## Character

Rust extension to the calibrant family proving BBAAj's runtime manifest-coverage
checks land their diagnostics. Three synthetic fixtures with deliberately aligned
or mis-declared manifests; one synthetic theurge-internal noop tabtarget the
fixtures invoke without cloud or filesystem side-effects. Self-contained — bash
driver pace consumes this but does not land here.

Intent-only docket. Catalog locked below; module placement, tabtarget naming,
manifest constant naming chosen at mount time.

## Scope

- Synthetic noop tabtarget, theurge-internal (rbtd-l* family — parity with
  rbtdrl_calibrant.rs).
- Three new fixtures in the calibrant family (Independent disposition):
  - calibrant-coverage-aligned: manifest declares noop, case invokes noop → Pass.
  - calibrant-coverage-undeclared: manifest declares nothing, case invokes noop
    → fixture-level FAIL via positive check.
  - calibrant-coverage-unused: manifest declares noop, case invokes nothing →
    fixture-level FAIL via negative check.
- Manifest entries + disposition tags.
- Rust unit tests pin only registration shape; verdict assertions live in the
  bash driver pace.

## Depends on

- BBAAh (calibrant-fixture-foundation) — needs rbtdrl_calibrant.rs and the
  calibrant-family scaffolding.
- BBAAj (theurge-manifest-coverage-check) — without the runtime checks landed,
  the failing fixtures pass instead of fail.

## Out of scope

- Bash assertions on exit code or stderr shape — sibling pace.
- Inclusion in the calibrant suite — these fixtures are standalone-invoked so
  the existing suite-fail-fast assertions remain clean.
- Cross-kit composition.
```

### Pace B: `calibrant-coverage-driver` (bash extension)

```
## Character

BUK-side testbench extension that black-box-drives the three coverage fixtures
from the sibling Rust pace through the rbtd binary. Asserts CLI exit codes and
stderr diagnostic shapes for the positive check (invoked-not-declared) and the
negative check (declared-not-invoked), plus the single-case exemption for the
negative check.

Intent-only docket. Section and case catalog below; case-file naming and
assertion helpers chosen at mount time.

## Scope

- New section manifest-coverage in rbtt_testbench.sh, four cases:
  - coverage-aligned-passes — Run.calibrant-coverage-aligned exits 0.
  - coverage-undeclared-fails — Run.calibrant-coverage-undeclared exits
    non-zero; stderr matches the positive-check diagnostic naming the offending
    colophon.
  - coverage-unused-fails — Run.calibrant-coverage-unused exits non-zero;
    stderr names the unused colophon.
  - coverage-single-case-skips-negative — SingleCase invocation of
    calibrant-coverage-unused's case exits 0 (proves the single-case exemption).
- Case files in Tools/rbk/rbts/ following BBAAi convention.
- Tabtarget(s) where manifest-driven generation yields them; testbench colophon
  registration.

## Depends on

- Sibling Rust coverage-foundation pace — needs the three fixtures and the
  noop tabtarget.
- BBAAi (calibrant-bash-blackbox-driver) — needs rbtt_testbench.sh and the
  case-file convention.
- BBAAj (theurge-manifest-coverage-check) — transitively, via the Rust fixtures.

## Out of scope

- Rust-side fixture work (sibling pace).
- New testbench infrastructure.
- Trace-file shape contracts beyond exit code + stderr substring.
```

### Sequencing notes

- Both paces become enrollable after BBAAh, BBAAi, and BBAAj all land.
- The single-case exemption case is the load-bearing one — it's the only assertion that catches an implementation that runs the negative check in single-case mode.
- These additions go into the calibrant family rather than a parallel test layer, so the existing testbench infrastructure carries them transparently.

Want me to slate both?
